title: Building a European Cloud Orchestration Platform within an Enterprise
gist: An enterprise team presented at KubeCon Europe how they built a cloud orchestration platform using Kubernetes Control Planes, Crossplane, and GitOps tooling to declaratively manage multi-cloud resources across AWS, GCP, and Azure, and subsequently donated the project to the EU-funded IPCEI-CIS initiative under the NeoNephos Foundation to strengthen European cloud-native sovereignty. Community building through inner-source collaboration and monthly tech talks was central to the adoption strategy.

insights.claim: An enterprise team at KubeCon Europe demonstrated using Kubernetes Control Planes with Crossplane to declaratively manage cloud resources across Google Cloud Platform, AWS, and Microsoft Azure from a single control point, eliminating per-tool lifecycle management.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: Control Plane methodology is gaining enterprise validation as a way to reduce multi-cloud complexity; Crossplane gains credibility as a key component of enterprise platform engineering.
insights.quote: We have tools such as Crossplane. It allows us to manage databases at Google Cloud Platform, Buckets at AWS, or Network Policies at Microsoft Azure.

insights.claim: The platform team combined External Secrets Operator, Kyverno, and Flux alongside Crossplane in a Kubernetes Control Plane, creating a uniform interface for secrets rotation, policy enforcement, and GitOps configuration management across cloud providers.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: The CNCF open-source tool stack is being validated for production enterprise multi-cloud management; platform engineering practices built on these tools reduce cloud provider lock-in.

insights.claim: OpenControlPlane enables companies to offer Control Plane instances to development teams with pre-installed and pre-configured operators, so developers order a Control Plane without needing to install or manage underlying tools themselves.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: Platform-as-a-service abstraction built on Kubernetes is a scaling pattern for enterprise platform teams; OpenControlPlane as a product layer reduces per-team onboarding cost.
insights.quote: Development teams order a Control Plane to get operators pre-installed and pre-configured; they do not need to worry about installing and managing all these different tools themselves.

insights.claim: The team found that modern cloud deployments involve many tools with different lifecycles — pipelines, CLIs, ticket-based configurations, and ClickOps — creating heavy burden on engineers and threatening development velocity.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: Operational complexity from tool sprawl is a recognized driver of platform engineering investment, supporting demand for unified orchestration platforms in the enterprise segment.
insights.quote: Our engineers have to lift the burden of maintaining and developing on the stack, taking up much time and attention from their development work.

themes.theme: European Cloud Sovereignty and Open-Source Governance

insights.claim: The enterprise cloud orchestration project was donated to the EU-funded IPCEI-CIS (Important Projects of Common European Interest - Cloud Infrastructure and Services) initiative and placed under the NeoNephos Foundation, a Linux Foundation Europe project ensuring vendor neutrality.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: EU government is actively funding and institutionalizing open-source cloud infrastructure as a strategic sovereignty initiative; this represents a structural long-term tailwind for non-US cloud platforms.
insights.quote: we could donate this project to the EU-funded project IPCEI-CIS (Important Projects of Common European Interest - Cloud Infrastructure & Services). This brings us closer to many different other projects that share the goal to strengthen Europeans cloud native sovereignty.

insights.claim: The Kubernetes Control Plane approach is positioned as a mechanism to ship software to private and sovereign clouds, reflecting growing enterprise demand for deployments beyond public cloud hyperscalers.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: Sovereign and private cloud deployment requirements — especially in regulated European industries — are creating a structural market for portable, standard-based orchestration platforms.
insights.quote: Software is no longer just shipped to a few data centers but more often to private and sovereign clouds.

insights.claim: The team built adoption for their Control Plane platform through monthly hybrid tech talks requiring close-to-zero prior Kubernetes knowledge, combined with inner-source collaboration and open-source release of most components.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: Bottom-up community building through education and inner-source is a proven enterprise platform adoption model, reducing the need for top-down mandates and increasing long-term stickiness.
insights.quote: We initiated a monthly tech talk, a hybrid event inviting people from different corners of the company to share commonly faced challenges.

insights.claim: Kubernetes Control Plane methodology significantly reduces manual operations work, handbooks, and other slow processes by making the entire desired state machine-readable and having Kubernetes Operators permanently hold orchestration knowledge.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: Declarative infrastructure automation via Kubernetes Operators is demonstrably reducing operational headcount requirements, making the platform engineering investment case clear.
insights.quote: Making the entire desired state well-defined in one machine and human-readable syntax, with Kubernetes Operators holding the knowledge of how to orchestrate and maintain them forever, significantly reduces the amount of manual operations work, handbooks, and other processes that used to slow us down.
